title: Community: Living the Faith Together
html:
The Big Question

Why can't I be a Christian all by myself? Why do I need others to live my faith?

It was around the year 197 AD. Tertullian, a lawyer converted to Christianity, was writing from Carthage one of the most beautiful observations ever made about the Church. The pagans, who despised Christians, could not help saying with a mixture of admiration and bewilderment: “See how they love one another” (Vide quomodo se diligunt, Apologeticum 39,7). And Tertullian added: “And how they are ready to die for one another.” What the Romans saw was not a brilliant doctrine or a spectacular temple. It was a community. People who shared bread, cared for widows, picked up abandoned children from the streets of Rome, visited prisoners without asking whether they were guilty or innocent.

This pagan amazement had its source in the first Christian community in Jerusalem. The Acts of the Apostles has preserved an unforgettable portrait: “They devoted themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer... All the believers were together and had everything in common. Selling their possessions and goods, they gave to anyone as he had need... They praised God and enjoyed the favor of all the people” (Acts 2:42-47). This was no utopia—it was a reality born of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost. And it was this community, with its visible and concrete love, that conquered the Roman Empire with no weapons other than charity.

The Catechism of the Catholic Church dedicates an entire section to the communal vocation of the human being. Because no one is an island. We were created for communion, to live in relationship with God and with others. “The human person needs to live in society. Society is not for him an extraneous addition but a requirement of his nature” (CCC 1879). I cannot be a Christian all by myself. I cannot save myself alone. Salvation is communal; faith is lived together; holiness is reached together. This lesson explores the fourth commandment—honor your father and mother—as the foundation of all social life; the principles of Catholic social teaching: the common good, subsidiarity, solidarity; and our obligations as citizens and as members of the Body of Christ.

Activity

Think of the community where you feel most at home. It could be your family, your group of friends, your parish, your catechesis group. What makes that place special? What does that community give you that you couldn’t have alone? Close your eyes for a moment and thank God for each person who is part of your “network of communion.” Faith is lived in a network. There are no solitary saints.

1. The Human Person Needs Community

The creation narrative gives us the first clue. After creating the man, God says something surprising: “It is not good that the man should be alone” (Gen 2:18). And He says this when man was already in Paradise, in friendship with God, surrounded by beauty. Something essential was missing: another human being. God creates Eve, and Adam exclaims: “This at last is bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh” (Gen 2:23). From the very beginning, we are made for relationship, for communion. The “I” only discovers itself before a “you.” And the “we” is the human reflection of the Trinity: a God who is communion of Persons.

The Catechism puts it this way: “The human person needs to live in society. Society is not for him an extraneous addition but a requirement of his nature. Through the exchange with others, mutual service, and dialogue with his brethren, man develops his potential; he thus responds to his vocation” (CCC 1879). Society is not a necessary evil or a calculated contract; it is a vocation inscribed in our nature. Modern individualism—that idea that each person makes themselves without owing anything to anyone—is an anthropological lie. No one makes themselves. We have all received life, language, culture, faith from others. We are heirs before we are owners, debtors before we are creditors, members before we are individuals.

Saint John Paul II, who experienced in his own flesh the loneliness imposed by totalitarian regimes—first Nazism, then communism in Poland—understood the value of community like few others. As a young man, he participated in the Rhapsodic Theater, an underground group of young people who kept Polish culture alive by reciting poetry in basements during the Nazi occupation. That experience marked him forever: community saves a people’s identity when power wants to destroy it. That is why, as Pope, he wrote that “man is the way of the Church” (Redemptor Hominis, 14) and that the human vocation is love, which is lived only in communion.

Connection

We live in the age of social media, but paradoxically there has never been so much loneliness. Millions of virtual “friends” do not replace a single real friend. The Church offers us something no algorithm can give: a real community, with flesh-and-blood people who share the same faith, who pray together, who help one another in difficulties, who celebrate and weep together. Are you taking advantage of the faith community within your reach? Or are you trying to live your faith alone?

2. The Family: The Original Cell of Social Life

The Catechism declares: “The family is the original cell of social life. It is the natural society in which husband and wife are called to give themselves in love and in the gift of life. Authority, stability, and a life of relationships within the family constitute the foundations for freedom, security, and fraternity within society” (CCC 2207). Everything begins in the family. There is no healthy society without healthy families. There is no living Church without Christian families.

The fourth commandment—“Honor your father and your mother” (Ex 20:12)—is the first commandment of the second tablet, which governs love of neighbor. This is no coincidence: before speaking of not killing, not stealing, or not lying, God speaks of the family. Because the family is the first school of humanity. In the family we learn to obey, to share, to forgive, to serve. We learn that we are not the center of the universe. We learn to love by first being loved.

Saint Paul comments on this commandment by adding a promise: “Honor your father and mother—which is the first commandment with a promise—that it may go well with you and that you may enjoy long life on the earth” (Eph 6:2-3). Honoring parents is not only about obeying when we are children; it is a permanent duty of gratitude, respect, and assistance. Even when parents grow old and become dependent, the commandment remains in force: to care for them as they cared for us.

Duties of Parents toward Their Children

The Catechism details the responsibilities of parents (CCC 2221-2231):

  • Education in the faith: Parents are the first evangelizers of their children. The home is the “domestic Church” where one learns to pray, to know Jesus, to live the sacraments.
  • Education in virtue: Teaching children to master their impulses, respect others, tell the truth, be generous. Moral education cannot be improvised: it requires example, perseverance, and loving correction.
  • Providing for their needs: Food, clothing, health, education. Parents have the grave obligation to work to support their family and to choose a school that respects their convictions.
  • Respecting their vocation: Not forcing children to follow the path parents chose for them. Accompany, advise, but respect each child’s freedom, especially in the choice of state of life and profession.

Duties of Children toward Their Parents

The Catechism also details what children owe their parents (CCC 2214-2220):

  • Respect and gratitude: “With deeds and words, honor your father and mother” (Sir 3:8). Gratitude toward those who gave us life is a duty of justice.
  • Obedience: While living in the parental home, children must obey their parents in everything that is for their good. Jesus himself “was obedient to them” in Nazareth (Lk 2:51).
  • Assistance in old age: “Son, take care of your father in his old age... even if his mind fails, be patient with him” (Sir 3:12-13). Returning the care received is a sacred duty.

In Depth

The fourth commandment does not apply only to the biological family. It extends to all relationships of legitimate authority: students with teachers, employees with employers, citizens with rulers. Where someone exercises authority for the common good, there is a duty of respect and cooperation. But be careful: the duty of obedience is not absolute. “We must obey God rather than men” (Acts 5:29). When a human authority commands something contrary to God’s law, we not only may disobey: we must. The Christian martyrs teach us that fidelity to God stands above any human power.

3. Nazareth: The School of Communion

The Holy Family of Nazareth is the model of every Christian community. There, in an insignificant village in Galilee, Jesus spent thirty years living the ordinary life of a family: working with Joseph in the carpentry workshop, helping Mary with household tasks, praying, sharing meals. The Son of God, who had created the universe, spent most of his earthly life in the silence, obedience, and hidden life of a family.

Pope Paul VI, during his pilgrimage to Nazareth in 1964, delivered a memorable address: “Nazareth is the school where we begin to understand the life of Jesus: the school of the Gospel... A lesson of silence, a lesson of family life, a lesson of work.” Nazareth teaches us that what is extraordinary about the Christian life is built in the ordinary. That holiness is not about doing spectacular things but about living with love the small things of each day in communion with others.

The Catechism gathers this truth: “The Christian family is a communion of persons, a sign and image of the communion of the Father and the Son in the Holy Spirit. In the procreation and education of children it reflects the Father’s work of creation” (CCC 2205). Every family is called to be a little Nazareth: a place where one learns to love as God loves, where forgiveness is practiced daily, where faith is passed on from parents to children like bread at the table.

4. Authority in Civil Society

Just as the family needs authority to function—parents who guide, protect, and educate—civil society also needs legitimate authorities. The Catechism teaches that political authority is not a mere human convention or a necessary evil; it has its foundation in human nature, created by God. “Every human community needs an authority to govern it. The foundation of such authority lies in human nature. It is necessary for the unity of the state. Its role is to ensure as far as possible the common good of the society” (CCC 1898).

Saint Paul expressed this clearly: “Everyone must submit himself to the governing authorities, for there is no authority except that which God has established. The authorities that exist have been established by God” (Rom 13:1). This does not mean that every ruler is good or that their decisions are always just. It means that the principle of authority is willed by God for the order and peace of society. Without authority, coexistence degenerates into chaos and the law of the strongest.

Nevertheless, authority is not absolute power. It is limited by the moral law and by its purpose: the common good. “Authority is exercised legitimately only when it seeks the common good of the group concerned and if it employs morally licit means to attain it” (CCC 1903). A regime that oppresses the innocent, denies religious freedom, or promotes laws contrary to human dignity loses its moral legitimacy. It is not authority: it is tyranny.

5. The Common Good

The central concept of Catholic social teaching is the common good. The Catechism defines it as: “The sum total of social conditions which allow people, either as groups or as individuals, to reach their fulfillment more fully and more easily” (CCC 1906). The common good is not the sum of individual goods, nor the annihilation of the person in favor of the State. It is creating the conditions so that each person can develop fully.

The Catechism distinguishes three essential elements of the common good (CCC 1907-1909):

  1. Respect for the person as such: Public authorities must recognize and protect the fundamental rights of each person: the right to life from conception, religious freedom, education, work, to form a family. Without these rights, there is no common good; there is only order imposed by force.
  2. Social well-being and development: Society must provide its members what is necessary for a dignified life: food, clothing, health, work, education, culture, information. This is not charity: it is justice. The universal destination of goods—a fundamental principle of Catholic social doctrine—means that God created the earth for everyone, not just for a few. Private property is legitimate, but it is subordinated to the universal destination of goods.
  3. Peace: A peace that is not merely the absence of war but “the tranquility of order” (Saint Augustine). True peace is the fruit of justice and love. It requires security, stability, and the rule of just law.

“They devoted themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer. Everyone was filled with awe, and many wonders and miraculous signs were done by the apostles. All the believers were together and had everything in common. Selling their possessions and goods, they gave to anyone as he had need. Every day they continued to meet together in the temple courts. They broke bread in their homes and ate together with glad and sincere hearts, praising God and enjoying the favor of all the people. And the Lord added to their number daily those who were being saved.”

— Acts of the Apostles 2:42-47

6. The Principle of Subsidiarity

Catholic social doctrine has developed a fundamental principle for the organization of society: subsidiarity. The Catechism formulates it thus: “A community of a higher order should not interfere in the internal life of a community of a lower order, depriving the latter of its functions, but rather should support it in case of need and help to coordinate its activity with the activities of the rest of society, always with a view to the common good” (CCC 1883).

In simpler words: what a smaller community can do by itself should not be taken away by a larger community. The family can educate its children; the State should not replace it. The municipality can manage its services; the federal government should not centralize everything. The parish can organize its pastoral work; the diocese should not smother it with bureaucracy. Subsidiarity protects the freedom and initiative of persons and communities.

Pope Pius XI, in the encyclical Quadragesimo Anno (1931), expressed this principle with unforgettable imagery: “It is an injustice and at the same time a grave evil and disturbance of right order to assign to a greater and higher association what lesser and subordinate organizations can do. For every social activity ought of its very nature to furnish help to the members of the body social, and never destroy and absorb them.”

Subsidiarity is the Catholic answer to both liberal individualism and statist collectivism. Neither the isolated individual nor the omnipotent State: persons in communities, communities in societies, societies united by the common good.

7. Solidarity: A Firm Determination for the Common Good

Alongside subsidiarity, Catholic social teaching proclaims the principle of solidarity. The Catechism defines it with Saint John Paul II’s words: “Solidarity is a firm and persevering determination to commit oneself to the common good; that is to say, to the good of all and of each individual, because we are all really responsible for all” (CCC 1939, citing Sollicitudo Rei Socialis, 38).

Solidarity is not a vague feeling of compassion for those who suffer. It is a virtue, a firm decision of the will. It is recognizing that the other—especially the poor, the suffering, the marginalized—is not a stranger but a brother. Saint John Paul II, who experienced the solidarity of the Polish people during the years of communist oppression, understood that solidarity is the force that transforms history. The Solidarity trade union, born in the Gdansk shipyards, took its name from this Christian conviction: together, the workers could defend their dignity against a system that treated them as disposable pieces.

The parable of the Good Samaritan (Lk 10:25-37) is the biblical icon of solidarity. To the question “Who is my neighbor?” Jesus changes the perspective: it is not about defining who deserves my help, but about my becoming a neighbor to the one who suffers. The Samaritan does not ask whether the wounded man is Jew or Samaritan, just or sinner, deserving or not. He simply sees him, is moved with compassion, and acts. Christian solidarity places no conditions.

Expansion

Solidarity is made concrete in the so-called “preferential option for the poor,” which the Catechism gathers (CCC 2443-2449). This does not mean that God loves the poor more than the rich, but that the Church, like Christ, must pay special attention to those who need it most. “Love for the poor is incompatible with immoderate love of riches or their selfish use” (CCC 2445). The corporal works of mercy—feeding the hungry, clothing the naked, visiting the sick—are not optional advice but demands of the Gospel. Jesus identifies himself with the poor: “Whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers of mine, you did for me” (Mt 25:40).

8. The Political Community and the Church

“Give to Caesar what is Caesar’s, and to God what is God’s” (Mt 22:21). With these words, Jesus established the legitimate autonomy of temporal realities and, at the same time, their ultimate subordination to God. The Catechism teaches that “the political community and the Church are autonomous and independent of each other in their own fields. Nevertheless, both are devoted to the personal vocation of man, though under different titles” (CCC 2245).

The Church does not seek to govern the State. It is not a political party, nor should it be. But it does have the right and duty to proclaim the moral principles that should govern social life: the dignity of the person, justice, the defense of life, the protection of the family, religious freedom. “The Church, by reason of her role and competence, is not identified in any way with the political community... Yet at the same time the Church has the right to pass moral judgments, even on matters touching the political order, whenever basic personal rights or the salvation of souls require it” (CCC 2246).

9. The Duties of Citizens

Being a citizen is not only about having rights; it is also about having duties. The Catechism lists the main ones (CCC 2239-2243):

  • Obeying just authorities: “Submit yourselves for the Lord’s sake to every authority instituted among men” (1 Pet 2:13). Just laws bind in conscience.
  • Participating in public life: Voting, staying informed, cooperating in associations that promote the common good. Democracy does not work without active citizens. Disengagement from politics is a form of irresponsibility.
  • Paying taxes: Not as theft by the State, but as a contribution to the common good. Taxes pay for schools, hospitals, roads, security. Tax evasion is unjust.
  • Defending one’s country: Legitimate defense of the nation, including military service under just conditions. The Catechism also recognizes conscientious objection to military service on religious or moral grounds, provided another service to the community is rendered.
  • Welcoming the foreigner: “The more prosperous nations are obliged, to the extent they are able, to welcome the foreigner in search of the security and the means of livelihood which he cannot find in his country of origin” (CCC 2241).

The Right to Conscientious Objection

The Catechism affirms clearly: “The citizen is obliged in conscience not to follow the directives of civil authorities when they are contrary to the demands of the moral order, to the fundamental rights of persons, or to the teachings of the Gospel” (CCC 2242). When a law is unjust—for example, a law permitting abortion, euthanasia, or any attack on life—the Christian not only may disobey: they must. Conscience stands above civil law. The apostles said it before the Sanhedrin: “We must obey God rather than men” (Acts 5:29).

10. Catholic Social Teaching: The Consistent Ethic of Life

From the encyclical Rerum Novarum of Leo XIII (1891) to our own day, the Church has developed a body of teaching on the just organization of society. This social doctrine is not a political ideology, neither left nor right. It is the application of the Gospel to social, economic, and political realities.

Saint John Paul II coined the concept of the “consistent ethic of life” (Evangelium Vitae, 1995): one cannot defend the life of the unborn and then disregard the life of the poor, the immigrant, the elderly, or the condemned prisoner. The defense of life is a seamless garment. The same logic that protects the child in the womb must protect the exploited worker, the terminally ill, the refugee fleeing war. There are no lives that deserve protection and others that do not. “Human life is sacred because from its beginning it involves the creative action of God and it remains forever in a special relationship with the Creator, who is its sole end” (CCC 2258).

The Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church summarizes its permanent principles: the dignity of the human person, the common good, subsidiarity, solidarity, participation, the universal destination of goods. These principles do not change with political fashions; they are demands of the Gospel for all epochs and all cultures.

11. Living the Faith in Community

None of the above is abstract theory. The Christian faith is lived in a concrete community: the parish, the catechesis group, the community of faith. I cannot be a Christian alone. I need the Word proclaimed, the sacraments celebrated, fraternal correction received, the witness of others who walk at my side. The Church is not a club I join when it suits me; it is the Body of Christ, of which I am a member. And a member separated from the body dies.

In Mexico we have beautiful examples of Christian community. Our Lady of Guadalupe not only converted millions to Christianity; she created a people, a community united by faith. The posadas, celebrated each December, are living in community Mary and Joseph’s journey: opening the doors of home and heart, sharing the ponche and the carols, remembering that hospitality is sacred. The tequio, the indigenous tradition of community work which the Church adopted and transformed, is a concrete expression of solidarity: the whole community gathers to build the school, repair the church, or help whoever is in need. No one charges; no one expects reward. It is done because we are community.

Biblical Connection: The Good Samaritan

Jesus does not give us an abstract definition of community. He tells us a story:

“A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and he fell among robbers, who stripped him and beat him and departed, leaving him half dead. Now by chance a priest was going down that road, and when he saw him he passed by on the other side. So likewise a Levite, when he came to the place and saw him, passed by on the other side. But a Samaritan, as he journeyed, came to where he was, and when he saw him, he had compassion. He went to him and bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. Then he set him on his own animal and brought him to an inn and took care of him. And the next day he took out two denarii and gave them to the innkeeper, saying, Take care of him, and whatever more you spend, I will repay you when I come back. Which of these three, do you think, proved to be a neighbor to the man who fell among the robbers?” He said, “The one who showed him mercy.” And Jesus said to him, “You go, and do likewise.” (Lk 10:30-37).

The Christian community is not defined by ethnic, political, or religious borders. It is defined by mercy. The Samaritan—the despised heretic—is the true neighbor. The community God wants is not a ghetto of people like me; it is a field hospital where every wounded person finds care, without anyone asking for their ID card.

For Reflection

  1. Which communities truly sustain you? Your family, your parish, your group of friends? What do you owe them? And what do you contribute to them?
  2. The fourth commandment calls us to honor parents. How are you living this commandment today, in your current situation? Is there something pending that you need to do for them?
  3. Subsidiarity says that what a smaller community can do should not be taken away by a larger one. Where do you see this principle violated around you? Where can you practice it better?
  4. The Samaritan saw the wounded man, was moved with compassion, and acted. Who is the wounded person at the edge of your path whom you see but refuse to see? What holds you back from acting?
  5. Saint John Paul II spoke of the “consistent ethic of life”: defending every human life from conception to natural death. How do you live this coherence? Is there any life—the unborn, the immigrant, the elderly, the criminal—that you find difficult to defend?

Key Idea

Key Idea: I cannot be a Christian all by myself. Faith is received in community, lived in community, and transmitted in community. The family is the first church; society is the space of love; the parish is the family of God’s children.

Cultural Wealth

The film “The Mission” (1986) and the Guaraní reductions — This cinematic masterpiece tells the true story of the Jesuit missions in Paraguay and Brazil during the 18th century. The Jesuits created communities where the Guaraní indigenous people lived their faith and developed their own culture, with baroque music that astonished Europe. When colonial powers decided to destroy those missions to carve up the land, the community resisted united. The film poses the hardest question: how does a Christian community defend itself against the violence of power? The Gospel answer—martyrdom and peaceful resistance—resonates in every scene.

Saint John Paul II and the Rhapsodic Theater — During the Nazi occupation of Poland (1939-1944), the young Karol Wojtyła was part of the Rhapsodic Theater, an underground group that recited Polish poetry in secret basements and apartments. The Nazis had banned Polish culture; the underground theater was an act of resistance and of faith. Wojtyła learned there that community—a handful of young people risking their lives to keep the word alive—can resist the most brutal tyranny. This experience shaped his entire pontificate: culture, faith, and community are inseparable.

Aristotle and man as the “political animal” — The Greek philosopher defined man as zoon politikòn: a political animal, a being made to live in the polis. But while Aristotle saw the polis as the ultimate end of human life, Christianity goes further: the earthly community is an image and preparation of eternal communion with God. The difference is decisive: we are not made only for the earthly city but for the City of God (Saint Augustine).

G.K. Chesterton and Distributism — The English writer proposed an economy based on widely distributed property, inspired by Catholic social teaching. Against capitalism (which concentrates property in few hands) and socialism (which concentrates it in the State), Chesterton defended “three acres and a cow”: that every family should have enough to be free. His vision of community rests on subsidiarity: landowning families, workers’ guilds, cooperatives. Power should be as close as possible to the people.

Mexico: tequio and posadas — The tequio is a pre-Hispanic tradition of community work that the Church adopted and transformed. In many indigenous and rural communities in Mexico, each family contributes its labor for common works: repairing the church, clearing the roads, building the school. No one charges; everyone benefits. It is subsidiarity and solidarity made flesh. The posadas, for their part, are Mexico’s feast of hospitality: nine nights going through the streets, asking for shelter, opening doors, sharing food and songs. Mary and Joseph seek a place to be born, and every home that welcomes them becomes Bethlehem. This is how community is built: by opening doors.

Archbishop Óscar Romero and the Salvadoran community — Archbishop of San Salvador, assassinated while celebrating Mass in 1980 for denouncing the injustices against his people. Romero understood that faith cannot be lived with one’s back turned to the suffering of the community. “The Church cannot remain silent before injustice,” he used to say. His martyrdom was the culmination of a life dedicated to building community among the poorest. Canonized in 2018, he is a model of a shepherd who knew how to be the voice of the voiceless.
